title: E-2 Visa for German Investors | Colombo & Hurd
content:
## E-2 Visa for German Investors
German citizens can live and work in the United States by investing in a U.S. business through the E-2 treaty investor visa. If you are researching the visa options available to you, the E-2 offers a direct route: it lets you enter America to develop and direct your own enterprise, without a fixed investment minimum or a path through employment sponsorship. Colombo & Hurd handles E-2 applications for German nationals through the U.S. Consulate in Frankfurt and change of status from inside the United States.

### Why Germany Qualifies for the E-2 Visa
Germany maintains a bilateral treaty of commerce and navigation with the United States. Eligibility is based on German citizenship, not country of birth or residence; naturalized German citizens qualify equally, regardless of their country of origin.
Unlike other visa categories, the E-2 does not require a fixed investment minimum or sponsorship from a U.S. employer. You enter America specifically to develop and direct your own enterprise. That hands-on requirement is what distinguishes the E-2 from passive investment vehicles.

## Find out if the E-2 visa is right for you.
Get a free evaluation today.

### Benefits of the E-2 Visa for German Investors
The E-2 offers several practical advantages that are particularly relevant for German nationals:
Strong bilateral trade relationship. German-owned businesses operating in the United States benefit from established commercial ties, shared regulatory frameworks in many industries, and a large German-speaking business community in multiple cities.
Five-year multiple-entry visa. German nationals typically receive a five-year multiple-entry E-2 visa, one of the longer validity periods among treaty countries. This gives you flexibility to move between Germany and the United States without reapplying each time.
Indefinite renewability. The E-2 can be renewed as long as your qualifying business remains active and continues to meet treaty requirements. There is no cap on renewals.
Work authorization for your spouse. Your spouse receives work authorization as part of their dependent E-2 status, no separate application required. They may work for any employer in the United States.

### German Investor Visa USA Requirements: E-2 Eligibility
To qualify for the E-2 visa Germany program, you must meet three core requirements:
- German citizenship. You must hold German nationality. Applications are processed on the basis of treaty eligibility, which is tied to citizenship, not residence.
- Substantial investment in a U.S. enterprise. Your investment must be substantial relative to the total cost of purchasing or creating the business. There is no statutory minimum, but the investment must be proportional to the business type and placed at risk in a real operating enterprise. Most successful E-2 petitions involve $100,000 or more, though lower amounts may qualify for lower-cost businesses. The adjudicative standard is proportionality, not a fixed dollar figure.
- Ownership and active control. You must own a controlling interest in the enterprise, typically at least 50%, and be entering the United States to develop and direct it. Passive or absentee investment does not qualify. You must hold a genuine operational role (as a managing director, CEO, or equivalent) and demonstrate hands-on involvement in business decisions.
The enterprise must also be “non-marginal”: it must generate income beyond what is needed to support you and your family, or have a credible plan to do so within a reasonable period.
For more details on E-2 visa requirements, read our complete guide: E-2 Visa: Complete Guide for Treaty Investors | Colombo & Hurd.

### Applying Through the U.S. Consulate in Frankfurt
The process begins before any documents reach the consulate. Colombo & Hurd works with German investors to structure the investment, prepare the business plan, and build the supporting petition package before anything is submitted.
German nationals applying from Germany apply for the E-2 visa through the U.S. Consulate in Frankfurt. The E-2 visa Frankfurt process has a distinctive step not shared by most other consulates: a company registration requirement that must be completed before your interview is scheduled.
The Frankfurt E-2 process follows this sequence:
- Company registration. Before scheduling an interview, your business must be registered with the consulate. This requires submission of both physical documentation and electronic copies. The registration package includes your business plan, financial projections, proof of lawful source of funds, evidence of investment, and corporate formation documents.
- DS-160 completion. Submit the online nonimmigrant visa application form with accurate information consistent with your registration package.
- Interview scheduling. Your interview appointment is scheduled after the consulate approves your company registration, not simultaneously. Plan for this sequencing when setting your timeline.
- Consular interview. Attend your in-person interview at the Frankfurt consulate with supporting documentation. Applications must be organized and concise; the consulate will not accept E-visa applications exceeding 30 pages.
Timeline guidance: Processing at the U.S. Consulate in Frankfurt typically requires approximately two months for company registration and interview scheduling, though this fluctuates based on application volume and consular workload. German applicants should plan for several months from initial filing to visa issuance and apply well in advance of intended travel dates. Colombo & Hurd has filed German E-2 applications through Frankfurt and understands what the consulate requires at each stage.

### How Long Can German Citizens Stay in the United States on an E-2 Visa?
German nationals typically receive a five-year multiple-entry E-2 visa. However, the visa stamp and your authorized period of stay are two different things and confusing them is one of the most common compliance errors among German E-2 holders.
Each time you enter the United States, U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) grants you a two-year period of authorized stay, recorded on your I-94 arrival record. That two-year clock starts on your entry date, not the date your visa was issued. The practical takeaway: keep an eye on your I-94 admission end date, and you’re in good shape.
German E-2 holders who plan extended U.S. operations have two options for maintaining status: depart and re-enter the United States before their I-94 expires (which resets the two-year period), or file an extension of status from within the U.S. if they are not departing. The visa can be renewed indefinitely as long as the qualifying business remains active.

### Business Types German Investors Pursue Through the E-2
The E-2 accommodates a broad range of business models. Colombo & Hurd has worked with German investors across several categories:
- Manufacturing subsidiaries and distribution operations. German precision manufacturing companies establishing U.S. production or sales arms
- Engineering and technical consultancies . Firms bringing specialized expertise in automotive, aerospace, and industrial sectors to the U.S. market
- Technology startups and software companies. Entrepreneurs launching U.S.-incorporated technology ventures
- Franchise acquisitions . Investors who prefer a proven business model with established brand support
- Professional and business services. Accounting, management consulting, and project management firms serving European clients operating in the U.S.
The investment substantiality analysis differs by business type. A service-based consultancy with low overhead requires a different documentation approach than a manufacturing operation with significant equipment costs. For a detailed breakdown of how substantiality is evaluated across business types, see our guide on E-2 Visa Investment Requirements.

### E-2 Success Story: E-2 Approved Through Frankfurt
One of Colombo & Hurd’s recent E-2 approvals involved a German citizen who established a Florida-based media production company specializing in digital content and educational materials focused on space technologies and exploration. The business had built a substantial online platform with over 226,000 followers and more than 41 million total views, averaging over 158,000 views per video. The investor committed substantial personal funds accumulated through prior entrepreneurial ventures and content creation, serving as CEO with hands-on involvement in strategic direction, production workflow, personnel management, and marketing. The application satisfied all key E-2 treaty requirements.

## Frequently Asked Questions
Yes. Germany has a treaty of commerce and navigation with the United States, which makes German citizens eligible for the E-2 treaty investor visa. Eligibility is based on German citizenship, not country of birth. Naturalized German citizens qualify equally, regardless of their country of origin.
German nationals applying from Germany go through the U.S. Consulate in Frankfurt. The process involves a company registration step with the consulate, submission of the DS-160 and supporting documents, and an in-person consular interview after registration is approved. German applicants should plan several months from initial filing to visa issuance.
German nationals typically receive a five-year multiple-entry E-2 visa. Each time you enter the U.S., you receive a two-year period of authorized stay, tracked by your I-94 admission date, not your visa expiration date. The visa can be renewed indefinitely as long as your qualifying business remains active.
There is no fixed minimum investment amount. The investment must be “substantial” relative to the total cost of the business. Most successful E-2 petitions involve $100,000 or more, but lower amounts may qualify for lower-cost enterprises. The key tests are proportionality to total business cost, irrevocable commitment, and funds at risk.
No. The E-2 is a non-immigrant visa with no direct path to permanent residency. However, German E-2 holders can pursue parallel pathways including the EB-2 National Interest Waiver for professionals whose work is in the national interest, employment-based sponsorship, or the EB-5 immigrant investor program, provided those applications are filed without violating E-2 status conditions.
Yes. E-2 investors can bring their spouse and unmarried children under 21 as dependents. Spouses are authorized to work in the United States for any employer incident to their dependent E-2 status, no separate work authorization application is required. Children may attend U.S. schools.
